title: Topical agents or dressings for pain in venous leg ulcers.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This is a Cochrane systematic review of randomised controlled trials evaluating topical agents or dressings for pain in venous leg ulcers. The paper contains no in vitro permeation/release experiments (no Franz cell, IVPT, diffusion cell, or membrane permeation data). All reported data are from clinical RCTs measuring pain scores, healing rates, and adverse events in human participants. There are no in vitro skin permeation or drug release measurements reported anywhere in the paper.

title: Toremifene interacts with and destabilizes the Ebola virus glycoprotein.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper (DOI: 10.1038/nature18615) reports structural biology (X-ray crystallography) and biophysical studies (thermal shift assays) of Ebola virus glycoprotein interactions with toremifene and ibuprofen, plus a cell-based pseudovirus fusion assay (BlaM reporter). There are no in vitro permeation, transdermal/dermal drug delivery, Franz cell, IVPT, or drug release experiments anywhere in the paper. All assays are either protein structural characterization, thermal denaturation, or cell-based viral fusion inhibition assays. This paper is entirely out of scope for dermal/transdermal permeation/release data extraction.

title: Fabrication of tri-layered electrospun polycaprolactone mats with improved sustained drug release profile.
raw_output_text:
Looking at this paper, I need to extract in vitro drug release data. The paper reports cumulative (%) IBU release from electrospun PCL fibrous mats over 62 days. The key data points come from Figure 7 and the text in the "Cumulative drug release analysis" section.

The paper explicitly states:
- Cumulative % at end of 62 days: 86.08% (12% PCL-IBU), 69.45% (10% PCLSW-1), 66.18% (10% PCLSW-2), 37.83% (12% PCLSW-1), 40.93% (12% PCLSW-2)
- Initial burst (Day 1): 66.43% (12% PCL-IBU), 57.87% (10% PCLSW-1), 53.61% (10% PCLSW-2), 27.48% (12% PCLSW-1), 15.70% (12% PCLSW-2)

The release study is a simple dissolution/release study (mats suspended in DPBS), not a transdermal/permeation study using skin or a membrane with a Franz cell. This is a drug release study from electrospun fibrous mats in a buffer medium - not a skin permeation/IVPT study. However, the schema includes "percent released" as an endpoint type and the study is an in vitro release experiment.

The device is not a Franz cell - it's a simple suspension of mat in DPBS with sample withdrawal. This is an in vitro release study from a matrix formulation.

While this is not a classic Franz cell transdermal permeation study, it is an in vitro release study of a drug delivery mat in DPBS medium, which qualifies as an in vitro release experiment. All five formulation groups (12% PCL-IBU, 10% PCLSW-1, 10% PCLSW-2, 12% PCLSW-1, 12% PCLSW-2) have explicit numerical percent released values at day 1 (burst) and day 62 (cumulative end). Release data from Figure 7 and the Cumulative drug release analysis section text. No Franz cell or skin membrane used - simple suspension in DPBS.

paper_scope.scope_notes: The paper reports in vitro drug release experiments for two ibuprofen-loaded cotton fabric formulations (Sample C with PLA-PEG carrier and Sample E with star-PLA carrier) measured over 14 days in PBS at room temperature. Release data are provided in Table 4 with both percentage and absolute (μg) values at 10 timepoints each. This is an in vitro release study (immersion in PBS, not a Franz cell/IVPT permeation study through a membrane). The cytotoxicity and anti-inflammatory sections are cell-based assays and are excluded per scope rules.

title: Enhancement of ibuprofen solubility and skin permeation by conjugation with l-valine alkyl esters.

paper_scope.scope_notes: Paper reports in vitro Franz diffusion cell permeation experiments through porcine abdominal skin for ibuprofen and six ibuprofen L-valine alkyl ester salts. Endpoints include cumulative permeated amount at 24h, flux (Jss), and skin accumulation. All are in-scope IVPT records.

title: Ibuprofen inhibits cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator-mediated Cl- secretion.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper investigates the effects of ibuprofen and salicylic acid on CFTR-mediated chloride secretion using Ussing chamber short-circuit current (Isc) measurements in epithelial cell monolayers (T84 cells, murine tracheal epithelium, human bronchial epithelium) and patch-clamp electrophysiology on excised membrane patches. These are electrophysiology/ion transport assays measuring ionic current (µA/cm²) or single-channel current (pA), not in vitro permeation/release experiments measuring drug transport across a membrane or drug release from a formulation. There are no Franz cells, IVPT setups, diffusion cells, or drug formulation permeation/release measurements reported. The paper is entirely out of scope for dermal/transdermal drug delivery data extraction.

title: Synthesis, in silico studies and biological screening of (E)-2-(3-(substitutedstyryl)-5-(substitutedphenyl)-4,5-dihydropyrazol-1-yl)benzo[d]thiazole derivatives as an anti-oxidant, anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial agents.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper reports synthesis of (E)-2-(3-(substitutedstyryl)-5-(substitutedphenyl)-4,5-dihydropyrazol-1-yl)benzo[d]thiazole derivatives and evaluates their in vitro anti-oxidant (DPPH assay), anti-inflammatory (egg albumin assay), and antimicrobial (serial dilution MIC) activities, along with molecular docking and in silico ADMET studies. There are no in vitro permeation or release experiments (no Franz diffusion cells, IVPT, or membrane diffusion studies) reported anywhere in the paper. The 'in vitro' Caco-2 and MDCK permeability values reported in Table 6 are computational/in silico predictions from the Pre-ADMET online server, not actual experimental membrane permeation measurements. Therefore, this paper contains no in-scope IVPT or release records.

paper_scope.scope_notes: Paper reports in vitro skin permeation experiments using two-chamber side-by-side glass diffusion cells with excised rabbit abdominal skin. Data include cumulative permeated amounts at 8h and steady-state flux values for escitalopram and five ESP ion-pair complexes (with BA, IB, SA, PABA, BSA) in IPP vehicle. All data are reported in Table III and Fig. 4.

title: Three-Compartment Model Analysis with Minimal Sampling Points in the Caco-2 Permeability Assay.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper reports Caco-2 cell monolayer permeability assays using a three-compartment model to calculate apparent permeability coefficients (Papp) for oral absorption prediction. These are cell-based transcellular transport assays, not dermal/transdermal Franz cell or IVPT experiments. The membrane is a Caco-2 cell monolayer (intestinal epithelial cells), not skin or synthetic skin-surrogate membranes. The study is entirely out of scope for dermal/transdermal drug delivery permeation/release data extraction.

title: Use of Silicone Membrane Permeation to Assess Thermodynamic Activities of Ionic Liquids and Their Component Cation and Anion.
raw_output_text:
I'll systematically extract all in vitro permeation data from this paper. The paper reports steady-state flux (fluxss) values for lidocaine and ibuprofen through a silicone membrane from various IL preparations (#1-9) and suspended aqueous solutions, measured in a horizontal two-chamber diffusion cell.

Key data points from the text:
- IL preparation #5 (equimolar): lidocaine flux = 3.99 ± 0.06 µmol/cm²/h, ibuprofen flux = 1.18 ± 0.05 µmol/cm²/h
- Suspended aqueous solutions: lidocaine = 7.92 ± 0.39 µmol/cm²/h, ibuprofen = 2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm²/h
- Figures 3a and 3b show cumulative permeation time courses (1-8h) for all preparations #1-9
- Figure 4a and 4b show observed steady-state permeation rates for all preparations #1-9

The flux values for individual preparations #1-4, #6-9 are shown in Figures 4a and 4b but not in tables. I'll extract the explicitly stated numeric values and note figure-derived ones.

paper_scope.scope_notes: Paper reports in vitro silicone membrane permeation experiments using a horizontal two-chamber diffusion cell for lidocaine and ibuprofen ionic liquid preparations and suspended aqueous solutions. Steady-state flux values are reported for IL preparation #5 and suspended aqueous solutions with exact numeric values; flux values for preparations #1-4 and #6-9 are shown in figures (Fig. 3 and Fig. 4) but not tabulated explicitly. All experiments are in vitro permeation through silicone membrane (75 µm, LINTEC) at 37°C with PBS pH 7.4 receiver.

title: Enhanced neurite outgrowth of human model (NT2) neurons by small-molecule inhibitors of Rho/ROCK signaling.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper reports in vitro neurite outgrowth assays using human NT2 neurons treated with Rho/ROCK pathway modulators (Ibuprofen, Y-27632, Fasudil, LPA, 8-Br-cAMP). The endpoints are neurite length (% of control) and percentage of neurite-bearing cells measured by immunofluorescence microscopy. There are no dermal/transdermal drug permeation experiments, Franz diffusion cells, IVPT setups, membrane permeation measurements, or drug release studies of any kind. This paper is entirely out of scope for pharmaceutical dermal/transdermal permeation/release data extraction.

title: Cyclooxygenase inhibition and the spinal release of prostaglandin E2 and amino acids evoked by paw formalin injection: a microdialysis study in unanesthetized rats.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper describes an in vivo spinal microdialysis study in unanesthetized rats measuring spinal release of PGE2-like immunoreactivity and excitatory amino acids following paw formalin injection. All experiments are in vivo animal studies (intrathecal microdialysis in live rats). There are no in vitro permeation experiments, Franz cell experiments, IVPT studies, or in vitro drug release experiments reported. The only 'in vitro' mention is a recovery calibration experiment for the dialysis probe (placing probe in ACSF containing PGE2 to determine % recovery), which is a method calibration step, not a drug permeation/release experiment in scope for this extraction task.

title: Overexpressed transient receptor potential vanilloid 3 ion channels in skin keratinocytes modulate pain sensitivity via prostaglandin E2.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper investigates TRPV3 ion channels in skin keratinocytes and their role in pain modulation via PGE2. While it contains in vitro cell-based experiments (electrophysiology and PGE2 release measurements from cultured keratinocytes), these are not dermal/transdermal permeation or drug release experiments using Franz cells, diffusion cells, or IVPT setups. The PGE2 release assays measure secretion from cultured cells into supernatant buffer, which is a cell secretion/signaling assay rather than a membrane permeation/drug release experiment. All in vivo behavioral data are animal pain/nociception studies. No Franz cell, IVPT, or membrane diffusion experiments are present. This paper is entirely out of scope for dermal/transdermal permeation data extraction.

title: Removal of Typical PPCPs by Reverse Osmosis Membranes: Optimization of Treatment Process by Factorial Design.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper is entirely about reverse osmosis (RO) membrane filtration for removal of PPCPs (ibuprofen, carbamazepine, triclosan) from water. It does not contain any in vitro skin/membrane permeation experiments (Franz cell, IVPT, diffusion cell) or drug release experiments relevant to dermal/transdermal drug delivery. All experiments involve RO membrane rejection/adsorption in a water treatment context, which is outside the scope of dermal/transdermal permeation/release data extraction.

title: In Vitro Assessment of the Antidiabetic and Anti-Inflammatory Potential of Artemisia absinthium, Artemisia vulgaris and Trigonella foenum-graecum Extracts Processed Using Membrane Technologies.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper reports in vitro enzyme inhibition assays (α-amylase, α-glucosidase, hyaluronidase, lipoxygenase inhibition IC50 values) and phytochemical/antioxidant characterization of plant extracts processed by membrane filtration. There are no Franz cell, IVPT, diffusion cell, or any skin/membrane permeation or drug release experiments. All 'in vitro' data are enzyme activity assays, not dermal/transdermal permeation or release studies. This paper is entirely out of scope for dermal/transdermal drug delivery permeation/release data extraction.

title: Dermatopharmacokinetics: factors influencing drug clearance from the stratum corneum.

paper_scope.scope_notes: This paper is entirely an in vivo dermatopharmacokinetic (DPK) study using tape-stripping of human stratum corneum to measure ibuprofen and propylene glycol disposition after topical application. All experiments were performed in vivo on human volunteers (ventral forearm), not in vitro Franz/diffusion cell permeation or release experiments. Rule 3 and Rule 4 explicitly exclude in vivo DPK/tape-stripping studies. No in vitro permeation or release data are reported.

title: Two-Photon Polymerisation 3D Printing of Microneedle Array Templates with Versatile Designs: Application in the Development of Polymeric Drug Delivery Systems.

title: Fluorinated PAMAM-Arginine Carrier Prodrugs for pH-Sensitive Sustained Ibuprofen Delivery.
raw_output_text:
I need to carefully analyze this paper for in vitro drug release data. The paper reports drug release experiments using dialysis membranes (SpectraPor® RC, MWCO=1 kDa) against PBS, measuring % IBU released at various timepoints. This is an in vitro release study (not Franz cell/IVPT permeation through skin), but it is a release experiment in scope.

Key experiments:
1. **IBU/PAMAM complexes (3-6)** - Physical encapsulation, release dialyzed against PBS for 24h, timepoints: 0, 0.5h, 1h, 2h, 5h, 8h, 24h, at physiological pH, 37°C
2. **IBU-PAMAM prodrugs (1-2)** - Covalent conjugates, release dialyzed against PBS for 130h, timepoints: 0, 2h, 5h, 8h, 24h, 48h, 52h, 72h, 96h, 120h, 130h, at pH 7.5 AND pH 8, 37°C

The data is primarily shown in figures (Fig. 3B and Fig. 4B) - not in tables. I need to extract what is explicitly stated in text:
- Fig 3B: Fast release within 5 hours for complexes 3-6 at physiological pH
- Fig 4B: ~80% release at pH=8 after 72h for conjugates 1&2; ~50% release at pH=7.5 after 72h for conjugates 1&2

Since numeric values at each timepoint are only shown in figures (not tables), I can only extract the text-stated values with confidence. I'll extract the explicitly stated values from text and note figure-based data with low confidence for key timepoints mentioned in text.

The experiments measure cumulative percent drug released over time for three formulations (SNM-IDE1, SNM-IBU1, SNM-MIC1) and corresponding neat drug suspensions.

The data are presented in Figures 7, 8, and 9. I need to extract values from these figures since no corresponding table is provided for the release data. The text provides key endpoint values at specific timepoints:
- SNM-IDE1: ~13% at 24h; neat IDE: ~9% max at 2h
- SNM-IBU1: 74% at 24h; neat IBU: 53% plateau at 4h
- SNM-MIC1: ~10% at 24h; neat MIC: ~5% at 24h

These are figure-derived values from figures 7, 8, 9. I'll extract all explicitly stated values from the text and figures.

title: Design and development of a novel fused filament fabrication (FFF) 3D printed diffusion cell with UV imaging capabilities to characterise permeation in pharmaceutical formulations.
raw_output_text:
I'll analyze this paper carefully. It describes a novel 3D-printed diffusion cell with UV imaging capabilities tested with an ibuprofen (IBU) gel formulation permeating through a silicone membrane.

The key permeation data is presented in Figure 11a, which shows "Average IBU amount in µg/mL permeated vs time" over 12 hours (720 minutes). The data points are read from the figure at the time intervals shown on the x-axis (0, 60, 120, 180, 240, 300, 360, 420, 480, 540, 600, 660, 720 min). The paper notes that data between 0-25 min falls below LOQ (17.69 µg/mL), and data between 440-720 min may not be reliable due to exceeding the calibration range (200 µg/mL).

The paper does not provide a table with explicit numeric values - all permeation data is from Figure 11a (graphical). Since the paper instructs to prefer table values over figure-digitized values, and no table exists, I must note these are figure-digitized. The paper only presents concentration in the receptor (µg/mL) - this is the "amount permeated" expressed as concentration in receptor, not a true cumulative amount permeated per area.

Given the figure shows approximate values that I cannot precisely digitize without risking inaccuracy, and following Rule 6 (do not infer missing numeric values), I will extract the data points that are clearly identifiable from the figure description and text, noting that exact values require digitization.

The paper text confirms: concentration gradually increased over 12h, 0-25 min below LOQ (17.69 µg/mL), 440-720 min potentially unreliable (>200 µg/mL). The figure shows timepoints at 0, 60, 120, 180, 240, 300, 360, 420, 480, 540, 600, 660, 720 min.
